food_name: Egg Tart, Hong Kong Style (Flaky Pastry Crust)
region_of_origin: Hong Kong, China (Cantonese dim sum tradition)
food_subgroup: Baked pastry tart with egg custard filling; flaky shortcrust pastry shell made with lard or butter, filled with smooth egg custard; 1 tart approximately 75g

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific entry for Hong Kong-style egg tart. No defensible exact URI available; closest parent would be a generic pastry or custard tart concept. Null assigned per ontology honesty policy.
ontology.foodex2_description: Pastries — FoodEx2 closest parent category for baked filled pastry items including tarts and custard pastries. No exact code exists for Hong Kong egg tart specifically.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A000E (Pastries) is the closest defensible parent. No specific code for Cantonese egg tart exists in the FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for Hong Kong egg tart or Cantonese custard tart.

serving.natural_language_portion: One Hong Kong-style egg tart weighs approximately 75g and provides around 200 kcal, making it a moderate-calorie dim sum snack or dessert item.

nutrient_source.title: Egg Tart (Hong Kong Style) — Health Promotion Board Singapore Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Singapore Food Composition Database contains an entry for Hong Kong-style egg tart. Values from the input record are consistent with HPB data for this item. Core macros and micronutrients adopted from this source. Copper, manganese, and vitamin K values are supplemented from USDA FoodData Central (SR Legacy, custard tart / egg tart analogues) at confidence 0.9 where HPB does not report these micronutrients.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Pastry and Custard-Based Foods — University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed analogous estimate
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Hong Kong-style egg tart exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 65 (medium) is estimated from the combined effect of shortcrust/flaky pastry (GI ~55, refined wheat flour base) and sugar-containing egg custard filling (GI ~65–70 for custard-type preparations). The high fat and protein content from eggs and lard/butter moderates the overall glycemic response. GL per 75g serving = (65 × 23.3g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 15.1. This is consistent with analogous custard tart entries in the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence: 0.9.

hypertension.guidance: One Hong Kong egg tart contributes approximately 90mg sodium, which is low relative to the 2000mg daily limit; sodium is not a primary concern for hypertension management with this food, though regular consumption of multiple tarts at yum cha should be monitored in the context of total daily sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium GI (65), GL 15.1 per tart, sugar 11.3g per tart from custard filling
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of 65 and a glycemic load of 15.1 per tart, one egg tart represents a moderate glycemic challenge; patients with Type 2 Diabetes should limit to one tart per occasion and avoid the common yum cha pattern of consuming two to three tarts, which would deliver 30–45g carbohydrate and 22–34g sugar in a single sitting.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 6.4g per 100g (4.8g per tart), cholesterol 145mg per 100g (109mg per tart), trans fat 0.1g per 100g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Each egg tart delivers 4.8g saturated fat and 109mg dietary cholesterol from the lard or butter pastry shell and egg custard; individuals with cardiovascular disease should treat this as an occasional item and avoid consuming multiple tarts in one sitting, as two tarts would provide nearly 10g saturated fat, approaching the daily limit for high-risk individuals.

llm_training.natural_language_description: The Hong Kong-style egg tart (蛋撻, Daan Taat) is a classic Cantonese dim sum dessert and Chinese bakery staple. It consists of a crisp, shatteringly flaky pastry shell — made from layered dough incorporating lard or butter — filled with a smooth, lightly set, bright yellow egg custard. The custard is prepared from eggs, sugar, and evaporated or fresh milk, producing a silky, mildly sweet filling with a delicate eggy flavour. The pastry shell is the defining feature of the Hong Kong style: it uses a water-dough and oil-dough lamination technique (水皮酥皮) to achieve multiple thin, crisp layers, distinguishing it from the Portuguese pastel de nata, which has a denser, more caramelised custard and a rougher, more rustic pastry. One standard Hong Kong egg tart weighs approximately 75g and provides around 200 kcal, 9.8g fat (4.8g saturated), 23.3g carbohydrate, 11.3g sugar, and 4.5g protein. The primary clinical concerns are: (1) saturated fat from the lard or butter pastry shell; (2) added sugar in the custard contributing to a medium GI of approximately 65 and a glycemic load of 15.1 per tart; (3) dietary cholesterol from eggs (approximately 109mg per tart). The egg tart is a treat food appropriate occasionally for most adults, but patients with Type 2 Diabetes or cardiovascular disease should limit to one tart per occasion and avoid the common yum cha pattern of consuming two to three tarts in a single sitting. The dish was adapted from British custard tarts during Hong Kong's colonial period (1840s–1950s) and became a cornerstone of Cantonese dim sum culture, widely available in Singapore, Malaysia, and Chinese communities globally.

confidence_reason: Core macros and primary micronutrients sourced from HPB Singapore Food Composition Database, which contains an exact entry for Hong Kong-style egg tart. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database methodology at confidence 0.9. Copper, manganese, and vitamin K supplemented from USDA FoodData Central analogues at confidence 0.9. All values are internally consistent and nutritionally plausible for this food type.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for Hong Kong egg tart is not reported in the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database or USDA FoodData Central for this specific preparation as of April 2026. water_g set to -1: moisture content not reported in the HPB source for this item. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero per HPB Singapore — egg tart contains no vitamin C-contributing ingredients). trans_fat_g of 0.1g per 100g reflects trace trans fat from lard or partially hydrogenated fat in traditional pastry preparation; confirmed present at trace level per HPB data. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Hong Kong egg tart exists in the University of Sydney GI Database as of April 2026. Portions: the canonical serving size is 1 tart = 75g per HPB Singapore; a secondary 60g portion size appears in some commercial contexts. Halal status assumes lard-free preparation (butter-based pastry); traditional recipes using lard would not be halal — this flag reflects the common Singapore commercial preparation. The input record contained duplicate portion entries which have been deduplicated in this output.
